entry.title: MAD-HTLC
entry.summary:
In discussing the strategic behaviors of miners within a blockchain environment, it is pointed out that myopic and non-myopic strategies have distinct impacts on earnings and dominance in game-theoretic terms. It is argued that in scenarios where the majority of miners are non-myopic but a minority adopts a myopic approach, the latter may actually end up benefiting more. This advantage arises because myopic miners, by confirming certain transactions (referred to as the Alice transaction), effectively prevent other transactions (the Bob transaction) from being processed. This action deducts fees from non-myopic miners who might have gained from delaying the Alice transaction in hopes of processing the Bob transaction for its higher fees.
The argument further elaborates that this dynamic leads to a situation where non-myopic miners are unable to impose similar costs on myopic miners, creating an unbalanced playing field. As a result, if even a single myopic miner successfully processes the Alice transaction, all non-myopic miners lose potential revenue from the Bob transaction. This scenario suggests that the myopic strategy not only provides immediate benefits despite its short-sightedness but also establishes a dominant position over non-myopic strategies within the population. This dominance could potentially discourage the emergence of non-myopic strategies among miners, leading to a prevalence of myopic behavior in such environments.
Overall, the analysis presents a compelling case for reconsidering the rationality of non-myopic strategies in certain game-theoretic contexts, particularly within mixed populations of strategic players like blockchain miners. The discussion points towards a deeper understanding of strategic interactions and their consequences in decentralized systems.
